md: ### Looking up, looking down

Most real-world applications of right-triangle trigonometry are some form of: **observer looks at an object** and measures an angle, then we use sine, cosine, or tangent to compute an unknown height or distance.
Two essential terms to master:
**Angle of elevation** — angle measured **upward** from the horizontal. ('Looking up at the top of a building.')
**Angle of depression** — angle measured **downward** from the horizontal. ('Looking down from a tower at a person on the ground.')
**These two angles are equal in magnitude** when measured between the same two points — by alternate interior angles between parallel horizontal lines.

blocks.title: Drawing the diagram first
blocks.body:
The hardest part of heights-and-distances problems is translating the words into the right triangle. Practice this:

**1.** Mark the observer and the object.
**2.** Draw horizontal line from the observer's eye (or feet, depending on setup).
**3.** Draw the line of sight to the object.
**4.** The angle between the horizontal and line of sight is the elevation or depression angle.
**5.** Drop perpendiculars to make a right triangle.
**6.** Label known and unknown sides; pick the right trig ratio.

With a clean diagram, the algebra is mechanical.

md: ### The three special angles (memorize these)
Almost every textbook problem uses one of $30°, 45°, 60°$. Their trig values:
| $\theta$ | $\sin\theta$ | $\cos\theta$ | $\tan\theta$ |
These come from the 30-60-90 and 45-45-90 special right triangles. Refer back to those lessons if needed.
### Single-observer template
**Setup.** An observer at point $P$ on level ground views the top of a tower of height $h$ at angle of elevation $\theta$. Distance from observer to base of tower is $d$.
**Right triangle.** Horizontal leg $d$, vertical leg $h$, angle at $P$ is $\theta$.
**Use tangent** (opposite over adjacent):
$\tan\theta = \dfrac{h}{d}$.
Given any two of $\{h, d, \theta\}$, solve for the third.
### Worked example 1
The angle of elevation of the top of a 50 m tower from a point on the ground is $30°$. Find the distance from the point to the base.
$\tan 30° = 50/d \Rightarrow 1/\sqrt{3} = 50/d \Rightarrow d = 50\sqrt{3} \approx 86.6$ m.
### Two-observer setup

**Setup.** Two observers on the same horizontal line view the top of a tower. The closer one sees angle $\alpha$, the farther one sees angle $\beta$ (so $\alpha > \beta$). Distance between observers is $D$.
Let tower height $= h$, distance from closer observer to base $= x$. Then:
$\tan\alpha = h/x \Rightarrow x = h \cot\alpha = h/\tan\alpha$.
$\tan\beta = h/(x + D) \Rightarrow x + D = h/\tan\beta = h\cot\beta$.
Subtract: $D = h(\cot\beta - \cot\alpha)$.
So $h = \dfrac{D}{\cot\beta - \cot\alpha}$, and then $x$ follows.

blocks.title: Worked example 2 — two-observer
blocks.body:
From a point on the ground, the angle of elevation of the top of a tower is $30°$. From a point closer by 30 m, the angle is $60°$. Find the tower's height.

**Setup.** Closer angle $\alpha = 60°$, farther angle $\beta = 30°$, $D = 30$ m between observers.

$\cot 30° = \sqrt{3}$, $\cot 60° = 1/\sqrt{3}$.

$h = \dfrac{30}{\cot 30° - \cot 60°} = \dfrac{30}{\sqrt{3} - 1/\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{30}{2/\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{30\sqrt{3}}{2} = 15\sqrt{3} \approx 25.98$ m.

md: ### Angle of depression — view from the tower
**Setup.** From the top of an $h$ meter tower, the angle of depression of a point on the ground is $\theta$. Find the distance from the base to the point.
**Key observation:** the angle of depression measured from the tower equals the angle of elevation measured from the point (alternate interior angles between parallel horizontal lines).
$\tan\theta = h/d \Rightarrow d = h/\tan\theta$.
**Example.** From the top of a $60$ m lighthouse, the angle of depression of a boat is $45°$. Distance from base of lighthouse = $60/\tan 45° = 60$ m.

blocks.title: Worked example 3 — depression from a moving aircraft
blocks.body:
An aeroplane is flying at altitude 1500 m. At a certain instant the angle of depression of a town on the ground is $60°$. After 15 seconds of horizontal flight, the angle of depression becomes $30°$. Find the speed of the aeroplane (in m/s).

**Setup.** Let the plane be at point $A$ initially and $B$ after 15 s; the town is at point $C$ on the ground; the foot of perpendicular from $A$ is $A'$ and from $B$ is $B'$.

From $A$: $\tan 60° = 1500/A'C \Rightarrow A'C = 1500/\sqrt{3} = 500\sqrt{3}$ m.

From $B$: $\tan 30° = 1500/B'C \Rightarrow B'C = 1500\sqrt{3}$ m.

Distance covered: $A'B' = B'C - A'C = 1500\sqrt{3} - 500\sqrt{3} = 1000\sqrt{3}$ m.

In 15 s. Speed $= 1000\sqrt{3}/15 \approx 115.5$ m/s ≈ $415.7$ km/h.

md: ### Tips for tricky problems
**1. Always draw the figure first.** Label everything clearly.
**2. Identify the right triangle(s).** Sometimes there are multiple (different elevation angles to the same point).
**3. Be careful with the question.** Sometimes asked for the *distance between the tower and observer*, sometimes *distance between two observers*, sometimes *height of tower*. Don't confuse them.
**4. Use the elevation-equals-depression rule** to convert tower-top views into ground-level views.
**5. When in doubt, use tangent.** It's almost always the right ratio because most problems involve only the vertical and horizontal sides — opposite and adjacent to the angle.
